topic: An employee finds out a junior colleague makes more money
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias leaned back in his ergonomic chair, the worn leather sighing beneath him. The fluorescent lights of the open-plan office hummed a familiar, monotonous tune. He was reviewing the quarterly budget projections, a task that usually settled a quiet calm in his chest. Then, a number popped up on his screen—a salary comparison for the junior analyst, Maya. A slow, cold dread began to pool in his stomach. He clicked the file open, his fingers hovering over the spreadsheet. The figures were stark. Maya’s compensation significantly outstripped his own. He didn't move for a long moment, just staring at the screen, the air suddenly feeling thick and hard to draw in. He quickly minimized the window, his breath catching in his throat. He turned back to the budget, forcing his gaze down to the data, meticulously tracing lines, trying to anchor himself back into the routine.

[story 2]

“Did you see the new compensation structure?” Sarah murmured to herself, swirling the lukewarm coffee in her mug. She watched Liam across the cubicle partition, his easy confidence radiating even from a distance. When the data finally materialized on her monitor—a comparison of pay scales—a strange stillness descended over her. Her hand tightened around the ceramic mug until her knuckles were white. It wasn't anger that flared, just a deep, settling weight. She felt a peculiar sort of equilibrium return, as if a heavy stone had finally been placed firmly on the floor. She took a slow, deliberate sip of coffee, the warmth spreading through her chest, and decided to focus entirely on the next task, her movements becoming precise and unhurried.

[story 3]

"It’s just numbers, right?" Mark muttered, rubbing the bridge of his nose. He watched Chloe walk past, her laughter bright and unconcerned. When he finally pulled up the internal HR report, the disparity between his salary and that of the new intern was undeniable. A sudden lightness washed over him, chasing away the earlier tension. He didn't confront anyone; he simply closed the laptop with a soft click. He stood up, stretched his arms above his head, letting out a long, quiet exhale. The office noise seemed distant, muffled. He walked toward the window, looking out at the city skyline, feeling utterly unbothered by the information, content in the simple fact that things, for now, were exactly as they were supposed to be.
